conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.

It sounds like you've been feeling pretty hopeless about quitting, like giving it another try doesn't seem worth it right now. What is it about quitting that feels like it's not worth the effort for you?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.

You've been worried about gaining weight for a long time, and it's clear that's a major concern for you. Can you tell me more about what happens when you're feeling anxious about your weight, is there a specific situation or trigger that makes you turn to smoking?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
It sounds like you're feeling stuck about quitting and worried about the challenges that come with it. What is going through your mind when you think about giving up cigarettes for good?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
You're still a bit hesitant about quitting, and that's understandable. What are some of the situations or emotions that make you reach for a cigarette when you're feeling uncertain about your goals?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.
Let's take a look at what might be underlying your resistance to making promises about quitting. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you say "let's get this over with"?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.
You feel that quitting has never worked out for you, and smoking has become a big part of your identity. What does quitting actually mean to you, and what would your life be like without cigarettes?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

You've tried several methods to quit, but unfortunately, they didn't work out for you. Can you think of a specific moment or situation that made you reach for a cigarette, even when you were trying to quit?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

I can sense your frustration with the people around you not understanding how hard it is to quit smoking. Have you ever thought about politely telling your friends and family why you decided to quit, and how it might help you stay committed to your goal?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

You're worried that all your past attempts at quitting have failed, which makes you doubt your ability to quit now. That's understandable, but I want to challenge that idea - do you think it's possible that you've learned something from each of those attempts, even if quitting didn't work out?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.
You're willing to take a small step, which is a great start. Would you be open to keeping a diary to track your triggers and cravings, so we can identify any patterns and find ways to manage them?

Honestly, I'm feeling really frustrated. I've been trying to quit for weeks now, but it's just not sticking. I've been smoking for about 10 years, and I know it's not good for me, but it's hard to stop.
You sound really frustrated, and it's great that you're acknowledging the difficulty of quitting. What is it that's motivating you to try to quit for real this time?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.

I can see how much your family means to you, Rachel, especially your niece and nephew. Would you be open to setting small, achievable goals, like staying smoke-free for a day, as a way to celebrate these little victories?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
Rachel, I love that you're already thinking about your past attempts to quit - that's a huge insight. Can you tell me what you think might have worked even a little bit from those previous attempts?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.

It sounds like you're feeling isolated in your efforts to quit, with many of the people around you still smoking. Would you be open to having a supportive buddy, like someone who's also quitting, to help keep you motivated and accountable?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.
Rachel, since you mentioned you've thought about joining a support group or talking to a counselor, what do you think would make you feel more comfortable reaching out for support like that?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
You're taking the first steps towards a healthier you, and I'm confident that you'll find the support you need.

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.

You're worried about the cost of quitting, which is a big concern. What are some of the things that go through your mind when you're considering quitting, and what makes it hard for you to take the first step?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

You've mentioned the cost and fear of failure being big obstacles, but you also mentioned not wanting to disappoint your family and friends again. Can you tell me more about what that disappointment would mean to you, and what kind of impact do you think quitting would have on your relationships?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.
So you're concerned about the cost and the fear of failure. What triggers your desire to smoke, like when or where do you usually light up?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

You're considering nicotine replacement therapy and also want to know about counseling and support groups. What kinds of situations make you want to smoke, like when you're stressed, bored, or with certain friends?
Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
You're starting to understand the risks associated with smoking. Now, let's talk about when and where you usually smoke. What situations or emotions trigger you to reach for a cigarette?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.

I'm glad you feel more hopeful about quitting this time. Now, let's talk more about what makes you want to smoke - is it something specific that happens when you're feeling stressed, like reaching for a cigarette when you're at work?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.
You're feeling a bit uncertain about why you're here and not too concerned about quitting yet. Can you tell me what a typical day looks like for you, and when you usually smoke?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?

I understand that trying to quit before wasn't successful and that you're concerned about gaining weight and giving up the social aspect of smoking. Those are definitely valid reasons to be hesitant. Can you tell me more about what the social aspect of smoking means to you - is it a way to connect with friends, feel more relaxed in certain situations, or something else?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.
I understand that you're worried about weight gain and social situations. Have you ever noticed what happens to you in those situations when you're not smoking?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

I think I understand why smoking is so tied to your identity and social life as a bartender. What if, for the next few weeks, you started avoiding situations at work where you know you'll be tempted to smoke, and instead focus on finding new ways to connect with your coworkers that don't involve smoking?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.
I'm glad you're open to exploring some alternatives. Can you think of a specific situation where you'd really like to be able to say no to a cigarette and stick to your decision to quit?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.
I can sense how frustrated and stuck you feel after trying to quit before, and it's completely normal to struggle. What is it about relapsing that's making you feel the most discouraged right now?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

It sounds like you felt a sense of pride and accomplishment when you cut down to a few cigarettes a day, but then the stress from work triggered you to go back to your old habits. What is it about quitting that's still important to you, even when it feels hard?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?

It sounds like you're open to exploring new options, but let's not focus on the specifics of those just yet. Can you tell me what motivated you to reduce your smoking in the first place, and what's keeping you from wanting to quit altogether?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.

You're open to considering new options, which is a great sign. Now, what is it about quitting smoking that's really drawing you in this time - is it more about your health, your family, or something else?
Yeah, that would be great.
You're open to exploring ways to manage weight gain, that's a great step forward. What motivated you to try to reduce your smoking in the first place, rather than quitting completely?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.

You're feeling hopeful about taking small steps towards quitting, like cutting down to just one cigarette break a day. What's driving you to make this change, is it something specific that happened recently or a general feeling that's pushed you to want to reduce your smoking?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.

You sound like you're feeling really overwhelmed and uncertain about quitting. What's been holding you back from quitting for good, even though you've been able to cut down to just one cigarette break a day?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.
I can sense your frustration and difficulty in quitting. When you're feeling overwhelmed and reaching for a cigarette, what kind of situations or emotions are you usually in at that moment?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

I can see that you feel like smoking helps you cope with your depression and anxiety, but also acknowledges it's making things worse. Have you considered writing down why you want to quit smoking, like for your mental health and well-being, to remind yourself of your reasons when you're feeling tempted?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

I think I understand how smoking helps calm you down temporarily, Rachel. Now, have you ever tried taking a short walk outside when you're feeling anxious or overwhelmed, just to see if it might help calm you down without reaching for a cigarette?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

Now that we're thinking about addressing the underlying issues, let's talk about how you can start to prepare yourself for those conversations. Can you imagine what it would feel like to take control of your mental health and reduce your reliance on smoking as a coping mechanism?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.
You've been smoking for years, and it sounds like it's become a familiar part of your life. How many cigarettes do you think you smoke per day, on average?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.
You've mentioned you've been smoking for years, but you haven't given me a specific estimate of how long. Can you tell me approximately how long you've been smoking?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.

Alex, it sounds like you're unsure about the benefits of quitting smoking because you've been smoking for years and you're still feeling okay. When you do smoke, what kind of situations or emotions do you usually find yourself in?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.

I can see that you're worried about how quitting might affect your mental health, especially your depression and anxiety. What specific situations or moments make you reach for a cigarette when you're feeling down or anxious?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.

Alex, you've been smoking for a long time, and it's a big part of your daily routine. What are some of the situations or activities where you usually smoke, and how do you feel when you're not able to smoke in those situations?
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.

You're worried about gaining weight if you quit, which makes you think of food and potentially eating more. How does the idea of potentially gaining weight affect your current eating habits or food choices?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.
I'm glad you're open to learning more, Alex. What's a typical day like for you, and when do you usually reach for a cigarette?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.

I can sense your frustration, it's normal to feel that way after trying to quit so many times. What happens right before you decide to light up, is there something specific that triggers you to grab a cigarette?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.

Your daughter's influence seems to be a strong motivator for you to quit smoking. How do you think you'd feel if your daughter asked you directly why you're still smoking despite saying you want to quit?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
It sounds like being around people who smoke can be a challenge for you. What kinds of situations or environments make you more likely to crave a cigarette?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit disconnected from the support you're getting online. Can you tell me about a time when you felt like you were getting the right kind of help and support to stay on track with your quit plan?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

You mentioned that cravings are a big challenge for you, especially during stressful work meetings and when you're driving. Have you ever considered taking a short walk or doing some light physical activity when you feel those intense cravings coming on?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
Remember, you've got the strength, support, and tools to overcome this challenge – I'm confident in your ability to move forward.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.

I can sense your frustration and disappointment, that must be really tough to feel like you've slipped back into old habits. What is it about quitting that you're hoping to achieve, something specific that's driving you to want to quit this time?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

I can see how hard it is for you to resist the urge to smoke when you're with friends and having a good time. Can you tell me what you think would be the best reason for you to quit smoking this time, something that would make it worth giving up the social aspect of smoking for you?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

It sounds like you're worried about feeling awkward or anxious in social situations without smoking. What's more important to you - being able to hang out with your friends while smoking, or being able to be around them in a way that feels healthier for you?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

Given your experience of quitting for two years in the past, can you think of anything from that time that might help you this time around, perhaps something you did that worked or that you could build upon?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

It sounds like you've become quite comfortable with smoking over the years and it's a big part of your daily routine. Can you tell me what made you decide to come in and try to quit now, when you've been fine with it for so long?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.
Alex, it sounds like you have a strong emotional connection to smoking, and it's been a big part of your life. Can you tell me what keeps you from wanting to quit, even though you've tried before?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

I understand you feel that quitting smoking won't make a huge difference in your life, and it's hard to imagine stopping without addressing other issues like your depression and anxiety that smoking helps with. Can you tell me more about how you think quitting smoking would impact your mental health?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.
You seem uncertain about the potential benefits of quitting smoking for your mental health, Alex. What's holding you back from considering that possibility?

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.
You've tried a few approaches before, but they didn't quite work out for you. Can you think of anything from those attempts that might have helped a little, even if it was just for a short time?

Well, I know it's bad for my health, and I'm worried about the impact it's having on my lungs and heart. But to be honest, it's also the stress and anxiety that comes with it. I use tobacco as a way to cope with my emotions, and I'm not sure what I'll do without it. I'm afraid that quitting will just make things worse.

I can sense your fear that quitting will make things worse, Rachel. Have you ever considered trying a short walk outside when you feel stressed or anxious, as a way to calm down without reaching for a cigarette?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.

I understand that you're worried about gaining weight and that's making it harder for you to quit. 

Considering you've been using nicotine gum, I'm wondering if you think it would be helpful to explore some alternative activities to replace smoking when you feel stressed or anxious?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.

You really enjoy the social aspect of smoking and miss the ritual of it. I'd like to try something that might help you find another way to relax and socialize without smoking. Have you ever considered trying a short walk with a friend or a loved one when you feel the urge to smoke?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.
Given your concerns about depression and anxiety, do you think it would be helpful to explore some strategies for managing those feelings that don't involve smoking?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.

Let's think about how we can make quitting feel more manageable when you're in social situations, like parties or gatherings with friends who smoke. Would you be open to planning ahead and coming up with a strategy for how you'll handle those situations when they come up?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.

You've been through a quit attempt before and it didn't go as planned, which can be really tough to deal with. It sounds like you're feeling pretty discouraged about it. Can you tell me more about what led you to start smoking again this time?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.

It sounds like social situations, especially with friends and alcohol, are a big trigger for you, and it's really challenging to resist those temptations. What is it about quitting that's really important to you right now - is it about improving your health, being a better role model for your loved ones, or something else?

I know it's bad for me, and I hate the way it makes me feel... guilty, anxious, and out of control. But at the same time, I really enjoy smoking. It's hard to explain, but it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation when we're together.

You're saying that you both enjoy and feel guilty about smoking. It's like it's a part of your life that you're not sure you're ready to give up. What's one thing that's changed for you since you last quit that makes you think you might be more ready to stay quit this time?
I guess so... I just don't know if I can do it. I feel like I've tried everything already.
I hear your doubts and frustration about quitting again. What's holding you back from giving it another try, despite knowing the negative effects of smoking on your health and well-being?
Yeah, okay. I guess that sounds like a good place to start.

You're feeling more open to exploring ways to quit, and that's a great step forward. What is it about quitting that's really important to you, and what would you achieve if you were able to overcome your addiction?
Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.
I understand you see smoking as a normal part of your life because your friends and family smoke too. How many cigarettes do you usually have in a day?

It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.

It sounds like you've had a pretty long history of smoking, starting in college, and it's become a deeply ingrained habit for you. What do you think you might lose or miss about your life if you were to stop smoking?

You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?

Alex, I can see that you're really worried about losing your social connections if you quit smoking. That must be a tough feeling to face. Can you tell me more about what a typical night out is like for you, and how smoking fits into that picture?

I guess not, really. I mean, I've tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't last. And yeah, it would be nice not to have to worry about finding a place to smoke or having to leave the bar to go outside. But I just don't know if it's worth it.
You're still feeling uncertain about whether quitting is worth it, Alex. What's holding you back from giving quitting a shot, even if it's just for a little while to see how it feels?
I guess so. But I'm not promising anything.
Alex, it sounds like you're willing to consider some resources, but you're still uncertain about quitting. What are some things that would make quitting feel worthwhile to you, right now?

Honestly, it's been tough. I quit a few weeks ago, but I'm still struggling. The anxiety and irritability are killing me. I feel like I'm on edge all the time. And working at the pub doesn't help. Everyone's always smoking and drinking, and it's hard not to join in.

I can see that quitting has been really tough for you, especially in your work environment. What do you think is the worst part about relapsing - is it the feeling of failure, or something else entirely?

The hardest part is dealing with my emotions. I feel like I'm a different person when I'm not smoking. I'm short-tempered, and I just want to lash out at people. And the cravings are constant. I'll be in the middle of a shift, and suddenly I'll smell smoke and it's like my brain is screaming at me to go grab a cigarette.

It sounds like you're really struggling with those emotional changes and cravings, and it's making you feel like you're not in control. What do you think is the most important reason for you to quit smoking, even when it feels so hard?

Yeah, I've tried some stuff. Deep breathing, meditation... but it's hard to do that when you're in the middle of a busy shift. And to be honest, I'm not sure I believe in that stuff. I just feel like I need a cigarette to calm down.

It sounds like you're feeling pretty skeptical about those strategies, and it's tough to see them working when you're in a stressful situation. What's holding you back from wanting to quit smoking, beyond just managing the cravings and withdrawal symptoms?
Hmm, that's a good point. If I'm not drinking, I might not be as tempted to smoke. But it's hard for me to imagine not having a drink after work. It's just what I do.
It sounds like having a drink after work has become a bit of a ritual for you. Are you worried that quitting drinking after work would change who you are or how you unwind?
Yeah, I think I could do that. One shift a week, that's doable.

You're feeling a bit more hopeful about quitting smoking now that we've talked about not having a drink after work, but what is it about quitting that's really important to you - is it your health, your family, or something else?
Yeah, that would be great. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I'm not alone in this anymore.
I'm glad you feel more hopeful about quitting. Now, what motivates you most to quit smoking - is it a health concern, your loved ones, or something else that's driving you?

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for a few months, but I started again. It's just so hard. I enjoy smoking, you know? It's one of the few things that helps me relax and takes my mind off things.
You feel like returning to smoking makes it harder to quit because you enjoy it, and it helps you relax. What's the usual amount of tobacco you use per day?

Well, I like the taste, the smell... it's just a whole experience, you know? And it's social, too. I work in events, so I'm always around people who smoke. We'll take breaks together and chat while we're smoking. It's a way for me to connect with my colleagues and friends. Plus, it helps me focus and calm down when things get crazy.
It sounds like smoking is deeply connected to your social life and helps you feel more relaxed. What's the most difficult part about thinking about quitting for you right now?

I don't know... I guess I just need to find something else that gives me that same feeling of relaxation and social connection. But it's hard, because nothing else seems to do the trick. And I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit. I've tried before and I ended up putting on a few pounds. That's just another stress I don't need.

You're worried about gaining weight and feeling stressed about trying new things. Have you ever considered that physical activity might help with both stress and weight management, and maybe even give you a new way to connect with others?

Now that you're interested in learning more about the health benefits of quitting, I want to ask: What specific aspects of your health are you most concerned about, and how do you think quitting smoking could impact those areas?
Yeah, that sounds doable.

Now that we have a quit date set, let's talk about how to handle those tough situations, like work stress or after meals, when you usually smoke. What do you think would help you stay on track when you're feeling really stressed or anxious?
Yeah, that's a good idea. My parents would definitely be on board with that.
It sounds like you're feeling more confident in your ability to quit and have a solid support system in place.
Yeah, that would be great. Thanks.
It's wonderful that you're taking proactive steps towards your well-being – I wish you all the best in your journey forward.
Yeah, I've been smoking for 20 years. I know it's bad for me, but it's just so hard to quit. I enjoy it, you know? It's one of the few things that helps me relax after a long day in the kitchen.
I can sense how much you enjoy smoking as a way to unwind after a long day. What is it about smoking that makes it feel like the best way to relax for you?

Well, it's the taste, the smell, the whole ritual of it. I like lighting up during breaks at work or after a long day. It's just something I look forward to. And, to be honest, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll lose that. It's something that brings me comfort. As for my medical history, I have high blood pressure and high cholesterol. I'm currently taking medication for those conditions.
You're really attached to the emotional comfort and ritual of smoking, aren't you? How do you think your life would be different if you didn't have to rely on cigarettes to feel that comfort?

I guess I want to be healthier, you know? I don't want to be stuck with a bunch of health problems when I'm older. And I want to set a good example for my kids. But, to be honest, I'm worried about how I'll cope with the stress of my job without smoking. It's already hard enough as it is.

It sounds like you're genuinely concerned about managing stress without smoking. Have you ever tried taking a short walk during your breaks at work, or doing something else relaxing that doesn't involve tobacco?
Yeah, I guess so. I mean, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to make sure I'm not setting myself up for failure again.
I understand your concern about setting yourself up for failure. What is one thing you think you could try differently this time that might help you succeed in quitting smoking?
Okay, that sounds like a good plan. I'm ready to take the next step.
You've taken a huge step forward, and I'm so proud of your courage and determination.

Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for 15 years, about 10-15 cigarettes a day. I've tried to quit a few times before, but it never seems to stick. To be honest, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know smoking is bad for me, but I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal for me personally.

You feel like it's not a big deal for you personally, but you've tried to quit before. What are some situations or emotions that you think might make you want to reach for a cigarette, even though you know it's not good for you?

Honestly, my girlfriend has been on my case about quitting, and I guess I just felt guilty enough to make an appointment. But like I said, I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. I enjoy smoking, it's something I look forward to after a long day of work. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to imagine giving it up entirely.

I sense you're torn between quitting for your girlfriend and enjoying smoking as a personal habit. Can you imagine a scenario where you're able to reduce your smoking, but not necessarily quit entirely, at least for now?

I think it's a combination of everything. I mean, I love the taste of cigarettes, I love the ritual of lighting up after a meal or during a break. And yeah, stress is a big one. When I'm feeling overwhelmed with work or personal stuff, smoking just feels like a release. But I guess if I'm being honest, it's also the social thing. All my friends smoke, and I don't want to be the odd one out.

You mentioned that stress is a big trigger for you, and you enjoy the release you get from smoking when you're feeling overwhelmed. Have you ever tried doing something physical to help manage stress, like going for a short walk or doing some stretching exercises?

I guess... I don't know, it's hard to say. I mean, I'd probably save some money, and my girlfriend would be off my back. But seriously, I suppose it would be nice to not have to worry about smoking-related health problems, even if I don't think they're a big deal for me personally. And maybe, just maybe, I'd feel more confident in my ability to handle stress without cigarettes.

Alex, I sense you're starting to see some potential benefits of quitting, even if it's still a bit unclear. You mentioned having more confidence in handling stress without cigarettes - can you imagine what that would look like in your daily life, what would be different for you?

Honestly, I'm a bit nervous about quitting. I've tried three times before and failed. But I'm really worried about my health, and my doctor told me I need to quit. I've been smoking for 15 years, about 10-15 cigarettes a day.
You're feeling nervous about quitting and worried about your health, which is really motivating you to stop smoking. What kinds of situations or emotions make you reach for a cigarette?

Well, I've been having some health issues, and my doctor said it's because of smoking. And, to be honest, I'm also worried about how it's affecting my appearance. I'm self-conscious about my weight, and I've seen friends who quit and gained a lot of weight. That really scares me.
You're worried about gaining weight after quitting, which is a big concern for you. What do you typically do when you feel stressed or anxious about your appearance?
Definitely managing my stress at work. I use cigarettes as a way to cope with the pressure and deadlines. I'm worried that if I quit, I'll be more irritable and my performance will suffer.

Honestly, no. I've always relied on cigarettes. But I'm willing to try something else. I just don't know what.

That sounds like a good plan. I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just really want to quit.

Look, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've been smoking for years, and I'm fine. My grandpa smoked till he was 85, so I'm not worried about it.

I guess a 2 or 3.

Yeah, I've heard that before, but I didn't think it applied to me.

Well, I want to be around for my kids, and I want to be able to play sports with them without getting winded.

Let's explore what situations or emotions might make you smoke more, Jake. When you're feeling winded or struggling to keep up with your kids, do you find that reaching for a cigarette helps you cope with those feelings?

Honestly, no. I don't really think about it that way. I mean, my friends and family are all cool with it, and it's just something I do. And at work, it's just a break thing. But I guess I do spend a lot of money on cigarettes... and sometimes I wish I didn't have to take so many breaks to smoke.

Probably around $200.

That's a significant amount of money, Jake. Given your concerns about your kids and wanting to be healthy for them, are you willing to try tracking your cigarette use and expenses for a week to see the full extent of what you're spending on cigarettes?
Yeah, I guess so.

Now that we've established your motivation to quit, let's think about how you'll handle those work breaks when you're feeling stressed. Who in your life could be that person for you, someone you can call when you're tempted to smoke, or someone who will support your decision to quit?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I quit for a few weeks six months ago, but I started again. It's just really hard when all my friends and family smoke. They're always asking me to join them, and it feels like I'm missing out if I don't.

I can sense how challenging it must be for you to resist the temptation of smoking when your loved ones are doing it around you. How do you think it would feel if you were able to stay smoke-free for a longer period, like six months ago when you initially quit?

I don't know, I just felt like I should quit, you know? My doctor was always on my case about it, and I didn't want to be a smoker anymore. But when I quit, I felt like I was losing my social life. My friends would go out for smokes during breaks at work, and I'd just sit there, feeling like an outsider. And then, one day, I just gave in and had one. Next thing I knew, I was back to my old habits.

It sounds like you're really struggling with feeling like you're losing out on social time with your friends. Have you thought about practicing saying "no" to cigarettes when they offer them to you, and reminding yourself why you decided to quit in the first place?
I don't know, man. I just don't think I'm at risk for all those health problems. I'm only 32, and I'm healthy as an ox. I don't think it's a big deal.

I see you're feeling pretty confident about your health. Have you ever considered replacing the social aspect of smoking with a different activity that you can do with your friends, like grabbing a non-smoking coffee or going for a walk together?

I guess I never really thought about it that way. I just figured I'd worry about it when I'm older. But if I'm being honest, it's scary to think about. My grandma died of lung cancer, and I don't want to go through that.

I don't know... maybe just trying to find some non-smoking friends or something? I don't know, it feels like such a small step.

Well, I think if I could just find ways to manage my anxiety and cravings better, that would be a huge help. And maybe if I could find some support, like a buddy or something, that would be great. I think it's just that I genuinely enjoyed smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, you know? My friends and I would smoke during breaks at work, and it was just a part of who I was. I guess it was just a way to take a break, you know? A way to relax and unwind. And it was something I shared with my friends. Now I feel like I'm stuck on the outside looking in, watching them smoke and feeling left out. It's hard to explain.

You're feeling really torn between staying quit and giving in to temptation when you're around others who are smoking. Can you tell me what's motivating you to stay quit, despite these tough moments?

Honestly, I think just having someone to talk to would help. And maybe some tips on how to manage the anxiety and cravings. I feel like I'm just trying to white-knuckle it right now, and it's not working.

Well, like I said, I'm healthy and active. I exercise regularly, I eat well... I just don't think I'm the typical smoker profile. And to be honest, I've tried quitting before and it was a disaster. I don't want to go through that again.

I think it's a bit of both, to be honest. I do enjoy the break that smoking gives me during my work sessions. It's a way for me to clear my head and recharge. And, I guess, it's just something I've always done. But, I don't know... maybe I'm just making excuses.

You've identified that your work sessions trigger a desire to smoke, which is a habit you enjoy for the break and relief it gives you. Would you be open to experimenting with a different way to take a break during work sessions, rather than reaching for a cigarette?

You're taking a powerful step towards understanding yourself, and I'm here to support you every step of the way.
